item.title: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
item.lesson: Seurat looked at Impressionism's spontaneity and decided to systematize it. Armed with contemporary color science, he spent two years and roughly sixty studies building this ten-foot canvas out of tiny separate touches of pure color — pointillism — meant to fuse in the eye at viewing distance into a shimmer no premixed pigment could match. The subject is Parisians at leisure on an island in the Seine: promenading couples, a woman fishing, a girl skipping, a monkey on a leash. But where Renoir's crowds breathe, Seurat's stand frozen — as formal as figures on an Egyptian frieze, which was exactly his ambition: 'to make modern people move past like figures on a Parthenon frieze.' Shown at the final Impressionist exhibition in 1886, it announced that the movement was over and something more architectural had arrived. Seurat died suddenly at thirty-one, leaving only a handful of major canvases; this one alone secured him permanence.
item.lookFor: Seurat later painted a border around the scene in dots of complementary colors, tuning the transition to the frame — and look for the small dog mid-bound in the foreground shade, one of the few creatures allowed to move.
